title: Move semantics — steal instead of copy
instruction:
Copying a vector copies all its elements. **Moving** it transfers ownership — O(1) instead of O(n).

`std::move()` casts to an rvalue reference, enabling the move constructor:

```cpp
vector<int> v2 = move(v1);  // v1 is now empty, v2 has everything
```
successMessage: Move transfers ownership — original is empty after move. No copying of all elements. This is why pushing large objects into vectors with push_back(move(obj)) is critical for performance.

title: auto and type deduction
instruction:
`auto` deduces the type from the initialiser. Especially useful for iterators and complex types.

`auto&` — deduced reference. `const auto&` — const deduced reference.
successMessage: auto shines for complex iterator and template types. The structured binding auto& [key, values] unpacks the map entry — much cleaner than .first and .second. Use auto for iterators; explicit types for simple local variables.

title: Structured bindings
instruction:
C++17 structured bindings decompose tuples, pairs, and structs:

```cpp
auto [x, y] = getPoint();           // pair
for (auto& [key, val] : myMap) {}   // map entries
struct Point{int x, y};
auto [px, py] = point;               // struct!
```
successMessage: Structured bindings eliminate .first/.second and get<0>(). Unpacking maps, tuples, and structs inline makes code dramatically more readable.

title: std::optional — null safety without nullptr
successMessage: optional communicates 'might not exist' in the return type itself. value_or() provides a default. No null checks scattered everywhere — the type tells you when to check.

title: Initializer lists and uniform initialization
instruction:
C++11 uniform initialization with `{}` works everywhere:

```cpp
int x{5};          // same as int x = 5
vector<int> v{1, 2, 3};
struct Point p{3, 4};
```

Narrowing conversions are a compile error with `{}` — safer than `()`.
successMessage: Uniform {} initialization works for everything. initializer_list lets functions accept {1, 2, 3} directly. {} prevents narrowing: int x{3.14} is a compile error — int x = 3.14 silently truncates to 3.

title: Build a modern utility library
instruction:
No starter code.

Using modern C++ features, build:
1. A `Chain<T>` class with `filter`, `transform`, `first` using `optional<T>`, and `toVector`
2. Use lambdas, auto, structured bindings
3. `first(predicate)` returns `optional<T>` — the first matching element

Test with a vector of integers:
- filter even numbers
- transform: multiply by 3
- find the first > 20
- collect to vector and print
hint: Chain<int> c(data); c.filter([](int n){ return n%2==0; }).transform(...).toVector();

codeChecks.message: Use std::optional for the first() method.

successMessage: A modern C++ utility chain using optional, auto, and lambdas. This is how ranges (C++20) work under the hood. Modern C++ is expressive, safe, and fast. Chapter 21 done!
